title: A Historian Reads Max Weber: Essays On The Protestant Ethic – By P. Ghosh
abstract: Ghosh, P. A Historian Reads Max Weber: Essays On The Protestant Ethic Harrassowitz Verlag Wiesbaden 2008 XIII-302 pp. €58 (hardback) This volume, the first in a new series –Studies in Cultural and Social Sciences– from an old and highly respected German publisher, is a very substantial new entry into ‘Weberiana’. It displays scholarship of remarkable width and depth, handling a huge body of primary and secondary sources with originality, forcefulness and subtlety (and occasional wit). Readers may however be warned that its subtitle is misleading: it ought to be Essays On ‘The Protestant Ethic’, not Essays On The Protestant Ethic. Put otherwise, it deals with the ‘making and makings’ of Weber's two essays of 1904–1905, and their joint, revised version of 1920. It does NOT assess the tenability of ‘the Weber thesis’ in the light of what is currently known about the genesis of modern capitalism, its purported ‘spirit’ (if any) and the latter's relationship (if any) with Protestantism. Instead, Ghosh is chiefly engaged in establishing, painstakingly and enlighteningly, the many and diverse sources on which Weber relied, generally in an imaginative and critical manner, in writing and later in defending and revising PE. He locates this masterpiece within a large body of scholarly writing, chiefly in German and English, from the ninteteenth century, as well as within Weber's own production. In the latter context, for instance, Ghosh rejects the view that Weber's work on religion after 1909–1910 marks a new turn with respect to PE itself. He argues, that ‘following a gradual evolution inaugurated by the promotion of rationality in Part II of the Protestant Ethic in 1905’, capitalism was replaced as a central focus by rationality and rationalization and thus ‘sidelined’. As a result Weber's construction of modernity would now be routed primarily through legal-rational bureaucracy and not capitalism, though he always supposed that the two were closely linked (pp. 89–90). Ghosh sharply contrasts his own approach, which he considers properly historical and thus empirical, with the widely shared exegetical approach to PE as a ‘canonical’ text (p. 3). Furthermore, he refuses to label his book as Werkgeschichte–‘the immediate history of a text’– and views it instead as Geschichte, a contribution to ‘the history of Weber's thinking’ (p. 3). I confess that tenability and significance of the distinction escape me – unless it simply means that Ghosh's research ranges masterfully over the entire Weberian corpus, including yet unpublished materials, thus well beyond PE and closely related writings. Basically, Ghosh tells us, ‘even in an author whom we may suppose to be well-documented, there is a great deal of subterranean life of which we are hardly aware, and. . . . any discussion of Weber's intellectual evolution must take account of this fact’ (p. 170). Furthermore, the book exhibits the author's familiarity with a very large part of the evergrowing body of Weberiana, and occasionally criticizes even the most authoritative contributors to that body, such as Wolfgang Mommsen, Schluchter, Hennis, or the editors of some volumes from the Gesamtausgabe. The results of Ghosh's pursuit of his topic (most of them originally published as essays between 2003 and 2005) are very impressive on their own terms. The question is, what about the prospective readers' terms? They may balk (I confess that I myself did) at the thought of tackling, say, the thirty pages of chapter six, ‘Max Weber's and German theological tradition: the case of Mathias Schneckenburger’. In fact, this discussion of Schneckenburger's (1804–1848) comparison between the traditions respectively of Lutheran and Calvinist theology reveals both considerable similarities with Weber's thinking, and considerable differences. In particular, unlike Schneckenburger, Weber – Ghosh emphatically states (p. 194) – was not a believer, and did not consider feasible or potentially valuable a contemporary merging between those two traditions. Other chapters persuasively argue even more important points, sometimes by dealing with texts other than PE itself, in particular (in chapter four) Weber's presentation at the St. Louis Congress of Arts and Science in September 1904, which Ghosh considers ‘the best synoptic presentation’both of ‘Weber's view of modern Germany’ and of his ‘views of modern capitalism’ (p. 88). Ghosh also insightfully compares Weber's views with those of Burkhardt's and William James's (chapters seven and eight respectively), emphasizing chiefly the distinctiveness of the former. Weber, for instance, thought little of Burkhardt's view of the Italian Renaissance as a most significant turning point in European intellectual development; more broadly, he discounted the historical significance of the early modern era. In a particularly valuable chapter (five), Ghosh discusses the role played in PE by Weber's knowledge of the Jewish tradition, which of course was to grow very considerably during his comparative research on the world religions. Toward the end of the chapter on Schneckenburger, the author proudly and provocatively asserts: ‘Although the “average” or “everyday” reader of the PE today may feel little instinctive desire to engage with German dogmatic theology, I hope it will now be clear that a right understanding of the text demands an awareness of the theological tradition from which so much of it derives’ (p. 196). Most readers may consider this a daunting challenge if they want to ‘carry on’ reading PE with Ghosh's blessings. Fortunately, he has done brilliantly much of the work he deems required – and delivered its result in this very book. In any case, even if they remain reluctant to undertake such work on their own, or to take advantage of Ghosh's performance, readers will find his occasional statements on Weber's theoretical position relevant to their own ‘average’ or ‘everyday’ reading. Ghosh, for instance, expressly criticizes the emphasis placed by other authors on the similarities between that position and Marx's. To him, the stress Weber's St. Louis address lays on ‘land and population’ indicates ‘the irreducible kernel of what might be called Weber's naturalistic materialism. . . . a whole world away from Marx’ (p.94). However, ‘Weber was, to an overwhelming degree, a theorist of Kultur; of man's inner, ultimate values and the variable rational means deployed to fulfil these’ (p. 83) This is a plausible view, and those who do not subscribe to it should consider how it is articulated and substantiated in this volume. A final point: the essays assembled in this book are apparently the by-product of a protracted effort to produce a new English translation and edition of PE. I hope that in completing that task Ghosh will take the trouble to consider and assess some of the literature produced, for better or for worse, by scholars conducting (once more) the ‘everyday‘ or ‘average’ reading (such as Gordon Marshall's books from the eighties) that, on the evidence of this book, Ghosh himself has very little time for.
